coredata.dc:title: An activity coefficient model for mixed-solvent electrolyte mixtures based on Gibbs–Duhem’s equation: A case study of mixtures of water, KCl and ethanol

coredata.dc:description: Thermodynamic properties of mixtures with several fluid components and electrolytes are challenging to model. Models are needed to develop and improve a wide range of electrochemical systems such as fuel cells, lithium-ion batteries, and processes with ion-exchange membranes. In the literature, activity coefficients are extracted using fundamentally different experimental methods that rely on electrochemical cells, vapour pressure measurements, solubility measurements, etc. The reference states of the activity coefficients obtained from these methods are likely to differ. This makes it difficult to combine or compare activity coefficient models from different sources. In this work, we present a method using Gibbs–Duhem’s equation for the development of activity coefficient models of mixtures that are based on experimental data from different methods. We use the ternary mixture of KCl, H2O and ethanol as example. First, empirical expressions are developed for the logarithm of the activity coefficients of KCl and ethanol. The expression for the activity coefficient of H2O is next derived using Gibbs–Duhem’s equation. The resulting three activity coefficient models are fitted to available experimentally data from several sources, generating linear and relatively low-complexity activity coefficient models. The empirical activity coefficient models are next compared to the electrolyte cubic plus association (e-CPA) equation of state. The models give saturation pressures in ternary mixtures that have average absolute relative deviations for water/ethanol of 8.3/3.4% for the empirical model and 11.8/3.3% for e-CPA. Experimental data reduction procedures for concentration cells, formation cells, and vapour pressure measurements are discussed, and the freedom to choose the reference state and the consequences are highlighted.

head.ce:title: An activity coefficient model for mixed-solvent electrolyte mixtures based on Gibbs–Duhem’s equation: A case study of mixtures of water, KCl and ethanol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thermodynamic properties of mixtures with several fluid components and electrolytes are challenging to model. Models are needed to develop and improve a wide range of electrochemical systems such as fuel cells, lithium-ion batteries, and processes with ion-exchange membranes. In the literature, activity coefficients are extracted using fundamentally different experimental methods that rely on electrochemical cells, vapour pressure measurements, solubility measurements, etc. The reference states of the activity coefficients obtained from these methods are likely to differ. This makes it difficult to combine or compare activity coefficient models from different sources. In this work, we present a method using Gibbs–Duhem’s equation for the development of activity coefficient models of mixtures that are based on experimental data from different methods. We use the ternary mixture of KCl, H2O and ethanol as example. First, empirical expressions are developed for the logarithm of the activity coefficients of KCl and ethanol. The expression for the activity coefficient of H2O is next derived using Gibbs–Duhem’s equation. The resulting three activity coefficient models are fitted to available experimentally data from several sources, generating linear and relatively low-complexity activity coefficient models. The empirical activity coefficient models are next compared to the electrolyte cubic plus association (e-CPA) equation of state. The models give saturation pressures in ternary mixtures that have average absolute relative deviations for water/ethanol of 8.3/3.4% for the empirical model and 11.8/3.3% for e-CPA. Experimental data reduction procedures for concentration cells, formation cells, and vapour pressure measurements are discussed, and the freedom to choose the reference state and the consequences are highlighted.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The thermodynamic properties of components in liquid and gaseous mixtures determine both phase equilibria and driving forces in transport phenomena. To represent these properties accurately is thus of high importance in a wide range of applications dealing with multi-component systems [1]. A system’s capacity to do work at constant temperature and pressure is described by the Gibbs energy, which is comprised of the chemical potentials of the components. These are functions that exhibit both ideal and non-ideal mixing behaviour [2,3]. The non-ideal, or excess, behaviour of the mixture is commonly described by activity coefficients, which measure the deviation from ideality.

Electrochemical systems comprise electrolyte mixtures, where the dissolved ionic species transport the charge in the fluid phases [2]. The thermodynamic activities of aqueous binary mixtures containing a single electrolyte are well described in the literature [3–5]. Multi-component systems with several fluid components and a single electrolyte have been investigated to a lesser extent, despite their central role in important applications such as lithium-ion batteries [6], hydrometallurgical processes [7], fuel cells [8], and ion-exchange membrane processes [9,10]. Particularly, the properties of one salt dissolved in two miscible solvents are of interest in several of these examples.

Moreover, as society progresses into a more circular economy, the recycling of metal compounds via hydrometallurgical methods becomes increasingly important. The hydrometallurgical processes using additive compounds that decrease the solubility of aqueous ionic species in order to induce precipitation/crystallisation, i.e. antisolvents, are particularly interesting [11–13]. Antisolvent precipitation methods exhibit high recovery rates, as well as adjustable precipitation selectivity and crystal structure [12,14,15]. Furthermore, a process for the recovery and reuse of the antisolvent involved in the hydrometallurgical procedure was described by Solberg et al. [10]. Modelling the chemical potentials of components in the mixtures is essential for the optimisation of such processes, and the current effort can be seen as the establishment of basic tools to explore new routes in hydrometallurgy.

In this work, we will study the activity coefficients in ternary mixtures of KCl, H2O and ethanol (EtOH). The investigations by Yang et al. [16] and Lopes et al. [17] utilised electrochemical concentration cells with ion-selective electrodes (ISEs), where the measured electric potential of the cell is directly related to the electrolyte chemical potential difference across the selective barrier/membrane of the ISE [2,18,19]. Measurements by Mussini et al. [20] and Dill et al. [21] used formation cells, which are electrochemical cells with two differing electrode reactions. The measured electric potential of such a cell comprises contributions from the differing electrode reactions and the activity of the electrolyte species that is consumed/created in the overall cell reaction. Vapour pressure measurements were also conducted by Yang et al. [16] for the KCl-containing mixed solvent, and mixtures without added KCl were measured by Foote and Scholes [22]. In such experiments, the vapour pressure of volatile components in equilibrium with the respective component in the liquid phase is measured. The vapour–liquid equilibrium allows for the determination of the activity of the liquid H2O and EtOH at various compositions.

The analysis and resulting salt activity coefficients differ between the studies, owing to the different reference states used for KCl. Yang et al. [16] used a constant reference state of infinitely dilute salt in pure water, while the remaining studies used the reference state as an adjustable parameter changing with the fraction of EtOH in the mixture. This choice gives a salt activity coefficient of unity in the infinitely dilute mixture, for all fractions of EtOH. However, it complicates the evaluation of the salt chemical potential gradient in the presence of EtOH fraction gradients. The importance of a consistent choice of reference states and state variables was also emphasised by Yang and coworkers [23]. We will compile and analyse the experimental results of these four studies as seen together. In the analysis, we will choose a single invariant reference state for the chemical potential of the electrolyte, and discuss the implications of the choice.

Efforts to model the activity coefficients of electrolyte systems are numerous, spanning from simpler empirical models to complex equations of state [24]. Notable examples include the electrolyte nonrandom two-liquid model (e-NRTL) [23,25], the extended universal quasichemical (UNIQUAC) model [26], speciation based models for mixed solvent electrolyte systems [27], the electrolyte cubic plus association (e-CPA) [28,29], and the Pitzer model based on a virial expansion of the excess Gibbs energy [30,31]. The e-CPA equation of state has shown promise in estimating thermodynamic properties in multi-component systems for a range of components and compositions [28]. Mixed solvent electrolyte solutions have also been modelled with the statistical associating fluid theory (SAFT) framework by parameterisation of binary activity coefficient data and extending to the ternary mixture by use of suitable mixing rules [32,33].

The advantage of using e-CPA to model activity coefficients is the general applicability to new mixtures and reliability in extrapolating to new conditions. Possible disadvantages is that it is challenging to implement and use, and its accuracy is limited by the functional form. Empirical activity coefficients models are much easier to implement and use, and they can be very accurate in regions where experimental data are available. Possible disadvantages are lack of thermodynamic consistency and ability to extrapolate well beyond the range where experimental data are available.

In this work, we will discuss data reduction methods for electrochemical and vapour–liquid equilibrium measurements with a focus on the importance of the reference state. Furthermore, we will demonstrate how empirical linear expressions for the activity coefficients can be constructed and combined with Gibbs–Duhem’s equation to improve their extrapolation properties in electrolyte mixtures. The linear regressions can be formulated in a relatively simple manner compared with more complex models, and the evaluation of gradients is straightforward. Using a ternary mixture of KCl, H2O and ethanol (EtOH) as example, we shall formulate linear empirical regression functions for the EtOH and KCl, and deduce a function for the water through the Gibbs–Duhem equation. Eventually the empirical relation will be compared to the e-CPA equation of state to evaluate the accuracy and applicability of the two approaches for the ternary system under investigation.

In this section, we start by presenting the basic theory of activity coefficient models (Section 2.1), before showing how the theory can be used to extract activity coefficients from two types of electrochemical cell measurements (Sections 2.2 and 2.3), and vapour pressure measurements (Section 2.4).

The formulae in this section will be specific to a system consisting of a monovalent salt dissolved in two volatile solvent components. The generic formulae for the electric potential of electrochemical cells are described by Kjelstrup and Bedeaux [2], and de Groot and Mazur [19]. General thermodynamic relations for various electrolyte mixtures have been described in detail by Harned and Owen [3]. The reader is referred to these sources for the framework necessary for application to other electrolyte mixtures.

Often the standard state is used as the reference state, but this is not always the most meaningful condition. For example, the standard reduction potential of a half-cell electrode reaction is typically tabulated for an electrolyte molality of 1 mol kg−1 [3]. Here, we choose the constant reference state of KCl as the infinitely dilute salt in pure water, and for the solvent components we choose the state of the pure component at standard temperature and pressure. The functional relation to other variable choices, such as mole fractions can be found in the Supplementary Information.

Salts dissolved in one or more solvents may crystallise if the salt concentration exceeds the saturation limit. At saturation, there exists an equilibrium between the dissolved and solid salt states that depends on the temperature, pressure and composition of the mixture [5]. In the case of a mixture of KCl, water and EtOH, the saturation equilibrium for the salt is expressed as:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvent vapour pressure in electrolyte mixtures
ce:section.ce:para:
The activities of each of the components of the mixed solvent with and without KCl were measured by Yang et al. [16] by vapour pressure measurements. Measurements of the vapour–liquid equilibrium without salt present were also conducted by Foote and Scholes [22]. The activities of the liquid solvents are related to the component vapour pressure when the two phases are in equilibrium. In the case of the water (and equivalently for EtOH):

The EtOH activity may also be shifted into an infinite dilution reference state, however as long as the reference state is kept constant it is inconsequential for the calculation of gradients of the chemical potential. The choice of reference state does, however, shift the value of the mixture excess Gibbs free energy.

In this section we present the two models that have been used to represent the activity coefficients, the empirical activity coefficient model (Section 3.1) and the e-CPA equation of state (Section 3.2).

More details on the model, such as the full expressions for the gradients of the chosen regression models, are available in the Supplementary Information.

Cubic-Plus-Association (CPA) is an equation of state for multi-component fluid systems that is based on the cubic Soave–Redlich–Kwong equation with contributions from molecular association phenomena such as hydrogen bonding [44–46]. The model has been extended to electrolyte mixtures, in the form of the e-CPA equation of state [28,47–50]. Maribo-Mogensen and co-workers described this model and the application to multi-component electrolyte systems in detail [28,48], and it has also been implemented in the Thermopack library [51]. The latter is here used for comparison with the empirical activity coefficient model described in Section 3.1. To improve the ethanol activity coefficient predictions of the e-CPA model, the CPA binary interaction coefficients used for water-ethanol have been regressed to the experimental data of Yang et al. [16] and Pemberton and Mash [52]. The new water–ethanol interaction parameters are
k
i j
= − 0 . 105
for the attractive energy and

= 0 . 0273
for the association energy. The KCl–ethanol interaction energy was regressed to the
ω

. We used the pure fluid CPA parameters and the KCl–water interaction energy of Maribo-Mogensen et al. [28].

These values can also be used to estimate the water activity by use of Gibbs–Duhem’s equation, and they were used to calculate the water activity coefficient as described in the Supplementary Information. All relevant thermodynamic data of the ternary mixture is compiled in the Supplementary Information, and the electric potential and vapour pressure measurements of systems containing EtOH are treated as described in the following sections.

All experimental data used in this work are available in the Supplementary Information. Furthermore, it also provides the procedure for converting the molal activity coefficients used in this work to the mole fraction variable set. In what follows, we shall compare the ability of two models to represent the activity coefficients of a ternary mixture of KCl, H2O and ethanol, an empirical activity coefficient model (Section 3.1) and the e-CPA equation of state (Section 3.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regression of the empirical activity coefficient model
ce:section.ce:para:
The regression and data reduction procedure outlined in Section 4 was used in developing the empirical activity coefficient model. This resulted in the coefficients listed with 95% confidence intervals in Table 1. The methodology presented in this work can also be extended to more ionic or neutral compounds, although the description of a mixture with N components requires activity coefficient data of at least
N − 1
of the components. Furthermore, the data should include a majority of the ternary composition space. The activity coefficients of KCl and EtOH were found to be well described by linear regressions with 5 adjustable parameters each; i.e., 10 in total for this ternary mixture. Modern electrolyte models such as e-CPA and e-NRTL, on the other hand, typically require few adjustable parameters which are fitted to data for binary mixtures and extrapolated to multi-component mixtures by use of mixing rules. One advantage of the empirical model lies instead in the linear form of the regression models, which makes for simple evaluation of activity coefficient gradients.

One drawback of the empirical model is how Gibbs–Duhem’s equation complicates the evaluation of
ln
γ
w
in the absence of water; i.e., for
ω
a
= 1
. This is due to our choice of the intensive state variables, which leads to the component activity coefficient calculated by Gibbs–Duhem to diverge when the concentration of that component goes to zero. If the state variables are instead chosen by dividing the amounts of moles of components by e.g. 1 kg of pure water (instead of the mixed solvent), then the thermodynamic activity of water will not diverge when applying Gibbs–Duhem’s equation as described in this work. The use of Gibbs-Duhem for one component’s activity coefficient may still aid the thermodynamic consistency of simple linear regression models in the remainder of the composition space. The intended use for the model treated in this work is ternary mixtures of EtOH fractions of
ω
a
= 0 . 9
or less; a range in which the model reproduces well the experimental measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The activity coefficient of the salt
ce:section.ce:para: The modelled activity coefficients of KCl with a constant reference state of dilute salt in pure water are shown in Fig. 3 for selected EtOH solvent weight fractions. While the empirical model is accurate for the whole range considered, the e-CPA equation of state displays larger deviations from the experimental data, although it gives activity coefficients for the salt that have qualitatively the correct dependency with respect to salt and ethanol concentration. Fig. 3(b) suggests that the agreement between e-CPA and the experimental results is best at low ethanol concentrations.

Activity coefficients derived from formation cell measurements by Mussini et al. [20] and Dill et al. [21] (
ω
a
= 0 . 2 , 0 . 4 , 0 . 6
) show good agreement with the concentration cell results by Yang et al. [16] and Lopes et al. [17]. The practice of analysing the formation cell results by use of the solubility equilibrium can therefore yield activity coefficients in good agreement with other measurement methods. An invariant salt crystal structure in equilibrium with the dissolved salt is required, however. It may also be useful in mixed electrolyte systems, if the crystal structures of the solid phases are known. Still, we find that analysing the formation cell measurements using
E
0 f
yield similar activity coefficient values and variances. For that it is necessary that
E
0 f
is the reference electric potential found by extrapolation of the electric potential to infinite dilution of salt in pure water. Deriving this value requires information on the activity of the potassium amalgam, but such data is available [20,21]. By going through the solubility equilibrium, the potassium amalgam activity is not needed, but instead the saturated KCl activity in H2O is required.

Many analyses of electrochemical measurements of the salt activity in mixed solvents use a variable reference state for the chemical potential of the salt [17,23,32]. This reference state ensures that
ln
γ
s
= 0
for infinitely dilute salt for all compositions of the mixed solvent. It can be a useful choice for the evaluation of the electrolyte behaviour in the dilute region, i.e., the Debye–Hückel behaviour. It is less useful when a spatial gradient of the chemical potential is required, e.g. in the description of transport phenomena in the electrolyte mixture [2]. The reason is that in this frame of reference,
∂
μ
s
0
/ ∂
m
s
= 0
but
∂
μ
s
0
/ ∂
ω
a
≠ 0
. The gradients of
μ
s
0
may also be calculated from the variation of
E
0 c
or
E
0 f
obtained in the data reduction, but here we have instead chosen a constant reference state of infinitely dilute salt in pure water. In such a case, any non-ideality is captured by the activity coefficient, rather than some parts being combined with the reference chemical potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity coefficient models of water and ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: The activity coefficients of H2O and EtOH calculated from the empirical model are shown in Figs. 4 and 5 respectively. The activity coefficients from the e-CPA equation of state behave similarly and have thus not been shown.

For each of these solvents, the pure liquids serve as the reference states, leading to
ln
γ
w
= 0
for pure H2O and
ln
γ
a
= 0
for pure EtOH. There is some disagreement between the model and the measured values of
ln
γ
w
at low weight fractions of EtOH and high concentrations of KCl. We note that the vapour pressure of EtOH and the total pressure of the ternary mixture were measured by Yang et al. [16] after freezing and subsequent thawing of the mixture. The freezing–thawing process of water and EtOH is known to exhibit some transient and composition dependent behaviour in the relaxation to equilibrium [54]. More measurements may be necessary to determine if the trend observed by Yang et al. [16] around
ω
a
= 0 . 1 − 0 . 2
is statistically significant.

The vapour pressures of an ethanol–water mixture without salt, and with a saturated salt solution are illustrated in Figs. 6 and 7 respectively. The predicted vapour pressure in the saturated solution at
ω
a
= 0 . 1
deviates from the measurement by about 8%, whereas Yang et al. [16] report the variation in repeated measurements as no greater than 3%. Omitting these deviations, the residuals of the empirical model are uniformly distributed across the state variables. Both models reproduce the saturation pressures with reasonable accuracy, with no salt present, and at saturated salt solution. A closer inspection of Figs. 6 and 7 reveals that the e-CPA equation of state slightly over predicts both saturation pressures. Comparing to all available saturation pressure measurements [16,22] gives for water/ethanol in ternary systems an AARD of 8.3/3.4% for the empirical model and 11.8/3.3% for e-CPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion of other models and consistency of the data
ce:section.ce:para: The measurements by Yang et al. [16] and Mussini et al. [20] were also investigated by Novak et al. using the electrolyte SAFT variable Mie (e-SAFT-VR Mie) equation of state, which had an average absolute relative deviation of 2.8% for the total pressure data, and an absolute deviation of 2.6% for the EtOH vapour fraction [32]. Comparatively, the current empirical activity coefficient model and e-CPA give deviations that are 1–5 times higher for the vapour pressures.

The SAFT model investigated by Novak et al. was developed using binary mixture data, and was not fitted to the ternary mixture measurements [32]. Similarly, the e-CPA model also aims to account for fundamental interactions between particles in the mixture, and it does so with a few adjustable parameters [49].

A multitude of mixed-solvent systems were also recently compiled and analysed with a benchmark database using e-NRTL [23]. This included the ternary mixture treated in this work, but only the measurements of Yang et al. [16] and Mussini et al. [20]. The e-NRTL model yielded average absolute values of the saturation pressure deviations/residuals of around 0.02, whereas the same values for the empiric model and e-CPA are around 0.1. The non-linear e-NRTL model proves an excellent model for mixed-solvent electrolyte mixtures.

Both e-CPA and e-SAFT-VR Mie are much more general and with a wider range of applicability than the empirical activity coefficient model. The main advantage of the empirical activity coefficient model is its simplicity combined with accuracy in the specific range that it was designed for.

The overlap of component activity coefficients from each data source evaluated in this work is adequate, but some variance is present. In Fig. 3 this can be seen by evaluating the activity coefficients calculated from measurements by Dill et al. [21] in comparison with those from Yang et al. [16]. The Pitzer–Simonson model used by Lopes et al., with reported residuals of mostly 0.6 mV or less [17]. The empirical activity coefficient model presented in this work has residuals around 5 times larger when recalculating the electric potential, but the residuals are still only about 2% of the measured electric potential. The average absolute values of deviations/residuals between model and measurements of the salt activity coefficients are 0.024 for the empirical model shown in Fig. 3, while the model is fitted to results from multiple different experimental investigations and methods.

Thermodynamic models for ternary mixtures where one of the components is an electrolyte are needed to develop and improve a wide range of electrochemical systems such as fuel cells, lithium-ion batteries and processes with ion-exchange membranes.

We have presented a method to develop an activity coefficient model for such systems based on linear regressions, using a ternary mixture of KCl in a mixed solvent of H2O and ethanol as example. First, empirical expressions were developed for the logarithm of the activity coefficients of KCl and ethanol. The expression for the activity coefficient of H2O was next expressed as a function of the activity coefficients of the two other components using Gibbs–Duhem’s equation. The coefficients in the empirical activity coefficient model were regressed based on data from a variety of experiments using concentration cells, formation cells, and vapour pressure measurements. Particular emphasis was placed on the data reduction procedure and the choice of reference state. We chose a constant reference chemical potential of KCl as the infinitely dilute salt in pure H2O, simplifying the future process for deriving gradients of the chemical potential.

The performance of the empirical activity coefficient models was gauged by comparing to available experimental data and the electrolyte cubic plus association (e-CPA) equation of state. e-CPA and other modern electrolyte models have a much wider applicability and fewer coefficients that need to be fitted than the empirical activity coefficient models, but also higher complexity. Both models gave a reasonable representation of the experimental data with the correct qualitative behaviour and dependency with respect to salt and ethanol concentrations. In particular, the models gave saturation pressures for water/ethanol in ternary mixtures with average absolute relative deviations of 8.3/3.4% for the empirical model and 11.8/3.3% for e-CPA.

The presented method to develop empirical activity coefficient models can in the future be leveraged to develop models with relatively low-complexity for other ternary systems. Future work can potentially also extend the methodology to include more components.
